results.possibleIdentity: Sophia M. Corwin (1915–1999), also known as Sophie Corwin, was a New York-based sculptor and painter. Her artistic career spanned several major 20th-century movements, including Cubism, Abstract Expressionism, and Minimalism. Corwin was particularly active during the 1960s and 1970s; she is listed in the New York State Council on the Arts (NYSCA) Annual Report for 1969-1970, a period during which the Council also awarded grants to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. She was a member of the 'Vectors' artist group and exhibited her work in various New York venues, including the Capricorn Gallery and Knapik Gallery. Corwin's formal training included studies at the Art Students League of New York and the Hans Hofmann School of Fine Arts. Her works, which transitioned from abstract expressionism to figurative abstraction in her later years, are documented in artist files at the Smithsonian American Art and Portrait Gallery Library.

results.possibleIdentity: Leah Williams Nolan (1901–1982) was an American artist, sculptor, and engineer who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Born in Kinsley, Kansas, she pursued a diverse career that bridged commercial art, engineering, and experimental fine art. In the 1930s, she gained recognition for creating intricate crepe paper figurines used in advertisements and magazine illustrations for publications such as Esquire, Look, and the Saturday Evening Post. She studied painting at the School of Design in Chicago under Laszlo Moholy-Nagy and maintained friendships with influential figures like Alexander Calder, Gyorgy Kepes, and Ross Parmenter. During World War II, Nolan utilized her technical skills to teach engineering drafting to women at the Illinois Institute of Technology and worked as a civilian engineer for the U.S. Army Air Force. After moving to New York City in 1950, she balanced commercial drafting with her painting career. In 1967, she retired from drafting to focus exclusively on art, experimenting with video, figure construction, and new painting techniques. She was significantly involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. Her work and correspondence are documented in the E.A.T. archives, particularly regarding the 1968–1969 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' held at the Brooklyn Museum. Her personal papers and artworks are held by the Kenneth Spencer Research Library at the University of Kansas and the Kinsley Library.

In the 1930s, she gained recognition for creating intricate crepe paper figurines used in advertisements and magazine illustrations for publications such as Esquire, Look, and the Saturday Evening Post.
During World War II, Nolan utilized her technical skills to teach engineering drafting to women at the Illinois Institute of Technology and worked as a civilian engineer for the U.S. Army Air Force.
After moving to New York City in 1950, she balanced commercial drafting with her painting career.
In 1967, she retired from drafting to focus exclusively on art, experimenting with video, figure construction, and new painting techniques.
She was significantly involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to foster collaborations between artists and engineers.
Her work and correspondence are documented in the E.A.T.
archives, particularly regarding the 1968–1969 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' held at the Brooklyn Museum.

results.possibleIdentity: Gary Williams is a physicist and technical documentarian who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He earned a doctorate in physics from The Catholic University of America and spent approximately 36 years as a physicist with the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). He is historically associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that facilitated collaborations between artists, engineers, and scientists. Records held at the Smithsonian Institution Archives (Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden) document his involvement with E.A.T., specifically through a file containing his 'Photographs of Production' related to the organization's collaborative projects and exhibitions during that era.

John Gary Williams (1946–2019) was an American R&B and soul singer best known as the lead vocalist of The Mad Lads, a group signed to Stax Records. He was active throughout the late 1960s and early 1970s, though his career was interrupted when he was drafted into the U.S. Army to serve in Vietnam (1966–1968). After returning, he continued recording with The Mad Lads until 1972, when he released a self-titled solo album on the Stax/Volt label. While primarily known as a musician, his active period and status as a collaborative artist in the Memphis soul scene align with the 1960s/70s timeframe, although a direct connection to Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is less established in public records than his musical career.

Gary Williams is a fine artist who graduated with a degree in Fine Art from the University of Arizona in 1968 and returned for master's studies in 1974. During his service in the U.S. Army in the late 1960s, he reportedly engaged in experimental large-scale 'earth drawings' using tank platoon maneuvers, which he likened to crop circles. His later career has focused on landscape, nature, and sky-themed paintings, which he exhibits and sells through various fine art platforms. His background in fine arts during the late 60s and early 70s makes him a contemporary of the E.A.T. movement.
